title: Split Tunnel Violation Detection
spl:
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:globalprotect") action="session_connect"
| lookup vpn_policy_requirements.csv group_policy OUTPUT required_tunnel
| eval violation=if(required_tunnel="full" AND (tunnel_type="split" OR match(lower(_raw),"(?i)split.?tunnel")),1,0)
| where violation=1
| stats count by user, group_policy, src
| sort -count
description: Flags sessions where observed routing or client flags indicate split tunnel when group policy mandates full tunnel ŌĆö complements UC-17.2.4/17.2.9 with explicit **violation** logic.
value: Flags sessions where observed routing or client flags indicate split tunnel when group policy mandates full tunnel ŌĆö complements UC-17.2.4/17.2.9 with explicit **violation** logic.
implementation: Align lookup with security architecture. Some vendors expose only group name ŌĆö normalize in transforms.
visualization: Table (violations), Bar chart (violations by group policy), Single value (violation sessions / day).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
ŌĆó Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_cisco-asa`, `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (GlobalProtect), `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, `Splunk_TA_juniper`.
ŌĆó Ensure the following data sources are available: VPN connect logs with `tunnel_type`, `split_include`, `default_gateway`.
ŌĆó For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection
Align lookup with security architecture. Some vendors expose only group name ŌĆö normalize in transforms.

Step 2 ŌĆö Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:globalprotect") action="session_connect"
| lookup vpn_policy_requirements.csv group_policy OUTPUT required_tunnel
| eval violation=if(required_tunnel="full" AND (tunnel_type="split" OR match(lower(_raw),"(?i)split.?tunnel")),1,0)
| where violation=1
| stats count by user, group_policy, src
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Split Tunnel Violation Detection** ŌĆö Flags sessions where observed routing or client flags indicate split tunnel when group policy mandates full tunnel ŌĆö complements UC-17.2.4/17.2.9 with explicit **violation** logic.

Documented **Data sources**: VPN connect logs with `tunnel_type`, `split_include`, `default_gateway`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_cisco-asa`, `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (GlobalProtect), `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, `Splunk_TA_juniper`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vpn; **sourcetype**: cisco:asa, pan:globalprotect. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Scopes the data: index=vpn, sourcetype="cisco:asa". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
ŌĆó Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
ŌĆó `eval` defines or adjusts **violation** ŌĆö often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
ŌĆó Filters the current rows with `where violation=1` ŌĆö typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
ŌĆó `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user, group_policy, src** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
ŌĆó Orders rows with `sort` ŌĆö combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 ŌĆö Validate
Compare with Palo Alto Networks Prisma Access or firewall manager for the same users, rules, and time window.


Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (violations), Bar chart (violations by group policy), Single value (violation sessions / day).
grandmaExplanation: We use network security tools to watch who accesses what, help prevent data leaks, enforce network isolation, and secure cloud and remote access.
